summary: Grupo Carso's purchase of TotalEnergies' 30% stake in a Gulf of Mexico block deepens private capital's role in offshore oil, even as Hacienda accelerates cuts to productive investment to control the fiscal deficit. Banxico's Jonathan Heath warns inflation risks still tilt upward, while BlackRock still sees investment grade holding despite USMCA review risk. Pemex's halt of Cuba exports after 33 years shows Mexico's caution under US pressure, as imports from Asia rose 42.4% despite tariffs and Honda ends EV production in Coahuila.

lead.h1: Grupo Carso buys TotalEnergies' 30% stake in a Gulf of Mexico oil block
lead.context: Carlos Slim's conglomerate expands into offshore oil exploration, adding private capital to a sector still dominated by Pemex.
lead.background: Pemex remains the dominant player in Mexican oil, but private firms operate blocks alongside it under contracts and joint ventures in the Gulf of Mexico.
lead.drivers: TotalEnergies is exiting Block 30, and Carso is expanding into segments of the oil and gas sector not under state control, following its earlier purchase of Fieldwood Mexico from Lukoil.
lead.implications: The deal extends Slim's holdings in a field operated by Harbour Energy, adding private capital to offshore exploration outside Pemex's direct control.
lead.next: The transaction requires approval from Mexican authorities.

items.headline: Banxico deputy governor says inflation risks remain tilted to the upside
items.context: Jonathan Heath said the balance of risks has not turned favorable despite June's slowdown, a signal the central bank may hold off on further rate cuts.
items.background: Banxico targets 3% inflation, give or take one percentage point, and adjusts its policy rate to hold inflation near that goal.
items.drivers: Heath pointed to better-than-expected inflation in the second half of June but said it remains unclear whether the decline will hold through the rest of the year.
items.implications: His comment that the balance of risks is not yet favorable suggests Banxico may proceed cautiously on further rate moves.

items.headline: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
items.context: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
items.background: Ernesto Ruffo was Mexico's first opposition governor, leading Baja California for the PAN from 1989 to 1995; huachicol fiscal refers to fuel-theft and tax-fraud schemes that have drawn growing federal scrutiny.
items.drivers: The FGR says the arrest follows a "high complexity" investigation into Ingemar, the customs company Ruffo founded, and it detained his business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro the same day.
items.implications: Ruffo has previously said his firm only handled customs paperwork, a defense now set against organized-crime and fuel-theft charges.
items.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

items.headline: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
items.context: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
items.background: Pemex is Mexico's state oil company, financially intertwined with the federal government and a longtime crude and fuel exporter, including to Cuba.
items.drivers: The halt follows sustained pressure from the Trump administration, which has threatened to intervene against Cuba's government.
items.implications: Exports to Cuba fell to zero in May, the first such stoppage since records began in 1993, showing Mexico's caution in balancing US pressure against its own foreign-policy stance.
items.next: Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending petroleum products despite Trump's threats.

items.headline: BlackRock says Mexico will keep investment grade despite USMCA review risk
items.context: The asset manager flagged that the annual USMCA review process could bring uncertainty for investors but said a downgrade of Mexico's sovereign rating remains unlikely.
items.background: Under USMCA's Article 34.7, Mexico now faces annual treaty reviews after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, and Mexico holds the last investment-grade rung at Moody's, kept there largely by the cost of supporting Pemex.
items.drivers: BlackRock's José Luis Ortega says the US refusal to auto-renew the pact extends investment uncertainty, but argues Mexico's non-confrontational government stance is building the basis for growth once conditions change.
items.implications: BlackRock considers a downgrade of Mexico's sovereign rating unlikely and does not see the annual review cycle as necessarily negative for growth.
items.next: Mexico will undergo annual USMCA reviews for the next ten years, during which the three countries could reach an agreement extending the pact's validity.

items.headline: Finance ministry accelerates cuts to productive investment to control fiscal deficit
items.context: Hacienda is cutting spending on productive investment ahead of the second half of the year in an effort to contain the fiscal deficit, according to the report.
items.background: Public debt has been rising and Pemex's finances require ongoing federal support, which competes with other spending needs.
items.drivers: Hacienda is leaning on cuts to productive investment, especially at Pemex, to offset the fiscal deficit even as SAT tax collection has improved.
items.implications: Physical investment in Pemex fell to its lowest level since 2008, with machinery capital down 45.7% year over year in the first five months, while social spending continued rising.

items.headline: Honda to end electric vehicle production in Coahuila
items.context: Weak EV demand is prompting Honda to halt its electric model in Mexico, following a similar pullback by Toyota and raising questions about EV manufacturing plans in the country's auto export belt.
items.background: Honda's Ramos Arizpe plant in Coahuila builds the Prologue, an electric SUV developed jointly with General Motors, within Mexico's auto export belt.
items.drivers: Weak US demand for electric vehicles is pushing Honda to end Prologue production and shift toward hybrids and combustion models, following a similar pullback by Toyota.
items.implications: The decision raises questions about the future of electric-vehicle manufacturing plans at Mexican plants built for the US market.
items.next: Production at Ramos Arizpe will stop by the end of this year, ending Prologue sales with the 2026 model.

items.headline: Mexico's imports from Asia grow 42% despite tariffs
items.context: Purchases from Taiwan, China and other Asian countries rose 42.4% through May while trade with the Americas grew only 4.5%, suggesting tariff pressure has not curbed Asian sourcing.
items.background: Mexico buys far more from Asia, especially China, than it sells there, and the government has applied tariffs on 11 countries without a trade agreement with Mexico, including China and Taiwan.
items.drivers: Imports from Taiwan rose 233.76% and overall Asian imports rose 42.55% through May, far outpacing 4.49% growth in imports from the Americas.
items.implications: Tariffs have not curbed sourcing from Asia, which now accounts for half of Mexico's total imports, according to the figures cited.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.52 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
